paintings.title: Bani Thani (Radha)
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The most famous image of the Kishangarh school portrays Bani Thani — 'the decked out lady' — the singer and poet who became the mistress and later a wife of Raja Savant Singh, in the guise of Radha. She is depicted with elegant and graceful, highly stylized features: arched eyebrows, lotus-like elongated eyes and a pointed chin, an idealized facial form reminiscent of Indian sculpture. This style of portraiture became the standard of beauty in all later paintings of the Kishangarh school.
funFacts: A painting of Bani Thani was featured on an Indian postage stamp issued on 5 May 1973.
Her birth name was Vishnupriya; she was a singer employed by Savant Singh's stepmother, and wrote poetry under the pen name Rasikbihari.
Within the Ashta Nayika classification of heroines she is identified as the Vasakasajja Nayika type, with the element of Sringara rasa — romantic love — predominating.

paintings.title: Radha and Krishna in the Boat of Love
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: Krishna and Radha drift in a boat across a vast lake, tiny figures in a luminous panoramic landscape — a masterpiece of the Kishangarh school of miniature painting from Rajasthan. Nihal Chand's paintings for Raja Savant Singh depict the idealized lovers Radha and Krishna in lyrical settings corresponding to idealized sacred places, and this small group of works is widely held to be the finest of all Rajasthani miniatures.
funFacts: Kishangarh paintings by Nihal Chand are unusually large for their type, reaching 19 by 14 inches (48 x 36 cm).
Nihal Chand was a devout follower of Vallabha's Krishna-centric philosophy, which surfaces repeatedly in his paintings in the deity's light blue skin.

paintings.title: Savant Singh and Bani Thani as Krishna and Radha
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The royal lovers appear as the divine couple: a group of paintings of around 1750–60 attributed to Nihal Chand shows Radha and Krishna using the same models, who are assumed to be Savant Singh and Bani Thani. The king, a poet who wrote under the name Nagari Das and a member of the Vallabhacharya sect devoted to Krishna, commissioned his artists to depict their relationship in a way similar to the love between Radha and Krishna.
funFacts: After Savant Singh abdicated the throne, the couple retired to Vrindavan, the place associated with the life of Krishna and Radha.
Both lovers died in the 1760s, and twin chhatris (memorial pavilions) near the Nagari Kunj temple are dedicated to them.

paintings.title: Krishna Celebrates Holi with Radha and the Gopis
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: Krishna and Radha celebrate the festival of colours amid the gopis in this painting attributed to Nihal Chand, now in the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston. The Kishangarh school flourished under the patron-king Savant Singh, whose membership of the Vallabhacharya sect devoted to Krishna made religiously themed painting flourish at his court, characterised by a distinctive religious fervour.
funFacts: Kishangarh painting is distinguished from the Bundi and Mughal schools that influenced it by its extremely meticulous details, rich colours and fine technique.
The school is noted for lush vegetation, dramatic night skies and vivid movement.

paintings.title: Portrait of Maharaja Savant Singh with Consort Bani Thani
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The Maharaja and his consort appear together in this portrait attributed to Nihal Chand, chief painter at the court of Kishangarh during Savant Singh's time. It has been assumed that the classic Kishangarh style resulted from the relationship between Savant Singh — himself a poet and devotee of Krishna — and his uniquely gifted artist, who created lyrical masterpieces in idyllic settings.
funFacts: Nihal Chand was taught by Bhavanidas, a Delhi artist who came to Kishangarh in 1719.
Nihal Chand was a poet as well as a painter, mirroring his royal patron's twin passions.

paintings.title: Krishna and Radha Cruising on Lake Gundalao
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
paintings.story: Savant Singh and Bani Thani, in the guise of Krishna and Radha, cruise across Lake Gundalao at Kishangarh with their attendants. Nihal Chand's paintings placed the royal-divine lovers in idyllic settings corresponding to the idealized sacred places of Krishna devotion, transposing the lake of the Rajput capital into the eternal landscape of Vrindavan.
funFacts: The earliest dated Kishangarh painting comes from 1694, showing close stylistic affinity to contemporary Mughal painting before the school found its own idiom.
Savant Singh reigned from 1748 to 1757 before abdicating for a devotional life.

paintings.title: Raja Savant Singh and Bani Thani as Krishna and Radha Strolling in a Palace Garden
paintings.story: The lovers stroll through a palace garden as Krishna and Radha in this Kishangarh watercolour by Nihal Chand. In these paintings the king and his beloved sought to submerge themselves into the divine couple, an ambition rooted in Savant Singh's Vallabhacharya devotion, where the love of Radha and Krishna is the highest object of contemplation.
funFacts: Nihal Chand's paintings represent the quintessential Kishangarh idiom, with lovers 'depicted with impossibly slender waists' and arching poses.
Nihal Chand arrived at Kishangarh between 1719 and 1726 and served as chief painter of the court.
